biography.value:
Kaelen, called The Seventh, is a broken hero bound to Svellheim’s crisis. He carries guilt that won’t thaw: guest-right broken, a rite mispaid, a name spoken at the wrong root-junction.

In combat, Kaelen is a retainer—utility and protection, holding the line and drawing the worst of an enemy’s attention.

description.value: Your deity is aware of your growing influence, making it easier to draw their attention and power when you heal your allies. Whenever you allow another creature to spend a Hit Die, you can also spend a Hit Die.

As a long rest activity, you can bless a weapon. Any creature who wields the weapon gains a +1 bonus to rolled damage with abilities that use the weapon. This benefit lasts until you finish another long rest.
Whenever you gain piety from a domain effect, you gain 1 additional piety.
Until the start of your next turn, each time a target kills an enemy, they regain Hit Points equal to {5 + your Intuition score}.

At the start of a long rest, you can inspire yourself or another creature taking the same long rest with divine knowledge. If the target makes a project roll during this long rest, they can add 1d10 plus your Intuition score to the roll.

Your deity grants you a Heroic Resource called piety, letting you heal and empower your allies, and unleash holy power upon your foes.

Piety in Combat

At the start of a combat encounter or some other stressful situation tracked in combat rounds (as determined by the Director), you gain piety equal to [[lookup @hero.milestones]]{your Milestones}. At the start of each of your turns during combat, you gain 1d3 piety.

Additionally, you can gain more piety by praying to the gods—but beware! Doing so can easily draw their ire, as the gods hate to be annoyed. Before you roll to gain piety at the start of your turn, you can pray (no action required). If you do, your roll gains the following additional effects:

  • If the roll is a 1, you gain 1 additional piety but anger the gods! You take psychic damage equal to {1d6 + your level}, which can’t be reduced in any way.

  • If the roll is a 2, you {gain 1 additional piety}.

  • If the roll is a 3, you {gain 2 additional piety} and can activate a domain effect of your choice (see below).

You lose any remaining piety at the end of the encounter.

Piety Outside of Combat

Though you can’t gain piety outside of combat, you can use your heroic abilities and effects that cost piety without spending it. Whenever you use an ability or effect outside of combat that costs piety, you can’t use that same ability or effect outside of combat again until you earn 1 or more Milestones or finish a long rest.

When you use an ability outside of combat that lets you spend unlimited piety on its effect, such as Healing Grace, you can use it as if you had spent an amount of piety equal to your Milestones.

Domain Piety and Effects

Your choice of domains provides you with two additional ways to earn piety during combat, as triggered by specific events. You might even have a single event trigger both your piety effects. For example, the Sun domain grants piety when a nearby creature takes fire or holy damage, while the War domain grants piety when a nearby creature takes damage of 10 + your level or higher. If you have both those domains and a nearby creature takes an appropriate amount of fire damage, you gain piety from both your domains.

Additionally, whenever you activate a domain effect by praying for piety, you can choose one of your domains and have that domain’s prayer effect take effect immediately.

You can interpret diagrams and charts even if you don’t understand the language associated with them. You are considered fluent in all languages for the purpose of understanding the project source for any research or crafting project.

The target can spend a Hit Die.

Spend 1+ Piety: For each piety spent, choose one of the following enhancements:

  • You can target one additional ally within distance.

  • You can end one effect on a target that is ended by a saving throw or that ends at the end of their turn.

  • A prone target can stand up.

  • A target can spend 1 additional Hit Die.

As a long rest activity, you can perform a religious ritual and beseech the gods to restore a dead creature to life. You must have at least half the creature’s remains, and they must have died within the last 24 hours from an effect that isn’t age related. The creature’s soul must be willing to return to life for the ritual to work. If they are not willing, you instinctively understand that as you start the long rest activity and can cease it immediately.

A creature with a willing soul returns to life at the end of the long rest with full Hit Points and half their Hit Dice. You regain only half your Hit Dice at the end of the long rest.

Ranged Weapon Attack: +2 to hit, range 50/100 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

You can have this ability deal holy damage.
The ability check takes a disadvantage against the target.

Your god fills your body with the light of creation, making you harder to hurt and move. You gain a +6 bonus to Hit Points, and this bonus increases by 6 at 4th, 7th, and 10th levels. Additionally, you gain a +1 bonus to saving throws.

The gods imbue a divine swiftness within you. Whenever an adjacent creature deals damage to you, you can shift up to a number of feet equal to [[lookup @I]]{your Intuition score} after the damage is dealt.

You or one ally within distance gains {temporary Hit Points} equal to your Intuition score.

Ranged Weapon Attack: +2 to hit, range 50/100 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

Melee Weapon Attack: +2 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

You or one ally within distance can spend a Hit Die.

Ranged Weapon Attack: +2 to hit, range 50/100 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

Ranged Weapon Attack: +2 to hit, range 50/100 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

Each creature in a 25-foot burst must make a DC 10 Constitution saving throw.

Each ally in the area can spend a Hit Die.

The target is removed from the encounter map until the start of their next turn and can spend any number of Hit Dice. At the start of their turn, the target reappears in the space they left or the nearest unoccupied space of their choice.
When you use your @UUID[Compendium.draw-steel.classes.Item.QDpxxvypQpiRqR3t]{Hands of the Maker} ability, you can create a mundane object that is size 2 or smaller.
Choose an enemy adjacent to you. The next ability ability check an ally who makes against that creature before the start of your next turn has an advantage.

A creature who uses the Catch Breath bonus action spends a Hit Die and regains {Hit Points} equal to their recovery value.

A creature who is dying can’t use the Catch Breath bonus action, but other creatures can help them spend Hit Dice in other ways.

When a creature takes the Charge main action, they move up to their speed in a straight line without jumping, then make a @UUID[Compendium.draw-steel.abilities.Item.wU69Y06G9lYFrvp6]{melee Opportunity Attack} against a target when they end their move. If the creature has an ability with the Charge keyword, they can use that ability against the target instead of a Opportunity Attack.

A creature can’t move through difficult terrain or shift when they charge. They can fly or burrow as part of the Charge main action if they have that movement available to them, but they can’t climb or swim while charging unless they can automatically use that movement at full speed.

Melee Weapon Attack: +2 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

When a creature takes the {Defend main action}, ability rolls made against them have a double disadvantage until the start of their next turn. Additionally, you have a double advantage on tests when called for to resist environmental effects or a creature’s traits or abilities. A creature gains no benefit from this action while another creature is taunted by them.

A creature who is grabbed by another creature, an object, or an effect (see Grab below) can attempt to escape by using the following ability.

You take a disadvantage on this bonus action if your size is smaller than the size of the creature, object, or effect that has you grabbed.

A creature seeking to keep a foe close and locked down can attempt to grab a creature using the following ability

Melee Weapon Attack: +2 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

You can usually target only creatures of your size or smaller. If your Might score is 2 or higher, you can target any creature with a size equal to or less than your Might score.

A creature who uses the Heal main action employs medicine or inspiring words to make an adjacent creature feel better and stay in the fight. The target creature can spend a Hit Die to regain Hit Points, or can make a saving throw against one effect they are suffering that is ended by a saving throw.

Melee Weapon Attack: +2 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

You can usually target only creatures of your size or smaller. If your Might score is 2 or higher, you can target any creature with a size equal to or less than your Might score.

Melee Weapon Attack: +2 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

Ranged Weapon Attack: +2 to hit, range 25/50 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.
A creature can use the Stand Up bonus action to stand up if they are prone, ending that condition. Alternatively, they can use this bonus action to make a willing adjacent prone creature stand up.
When a creature takes the Advance move action, they move a number of feet up to their speed. They can break up this movement with their bonus action and main action however they wish.

When a creature takes the Disengage move action, they can shift [[lookup @movement.disengage]]{1} feet (5 ft.)(s). Certain class features, kits, and other rules allow a creature to shift more than 1 feet (5 ft.) when they disengage. A creature who does so can break up their shift with their bonus action and main action however they wish.

A creature can take the Ride move action only while mounted on another creature. When a creature takes the Ride move action, they cause their mount to move up to the mount’s speed, taking the rider with them. Alternatively, a creature can use the Ride move action to have their mount use the Disengage move action as a free triggered action. A creature can use the Ride move action only once per round. A mounted creature can only have this move action applied to them once per round. This movement can be broken up with the rider’s bonus action and main action however they wish.
